interview_metadata.interviewee_title: Civil Rights Activist and Journalist; SNCC and Delta Ministry Freedom Corps Veteran
interview_metadata.interviewer_affiliation: Library of Congress / Civil Rights History Project

main_summary.summary:
Juadine Henderson, born in Batesville, Mississippi (Panola County), recounts a half-century arc that begins on a small Black-owned farm and moves outward through SNCC's Mississippi Delta voter-registration project, the 1968 Poor People's Campaign, the Drum and Spear Bookstore in Washington, D.C., and a 28-year career in journalism. Henderson's father died when she was about four or five; she grew up with her mother and, from age ten, a stepfather on an 80-acre family farm in a section the community called 'Section 7.' Her political education began before she had a name for it: working in her mother's segregated kitchen at Brown's Cafe at age eight, reading Jet and Look magazines about the Emmett Till murder, discovering her mother's NAACP card she was told never to speak of, and hearing Reverend Middleton Godfrey teach Black history (including Crispus Attucks) on the church steps every other Sunday after services.

Her formal entry into the movement came at Thanksgiving 1962, when SNCC field secretary Frank Smith visited Batesville via her cousin at Rust College and described the civil rights movement to her. By June 1963 she had traveled with her cousin Jimmy, friend Faye, and John Hardy to a SNCC voter-registration workshop in Greenwood, Mississippi, where she heard the news of Medgar Evers's assassination, learned to canvas, met Willie Peacock and John O'Neal, and heard that Fannie Lou Hamer, June Johnson, and Euvester Simpson were missing (later understood as the Winona jail beatings, prompting Lawrence Guyot's search). She returned to Batesville and began organizing local young people, played 'Oh Freedom' instead of 'Amazing Grace' at Concord Missionary Baptist Church, and joined sit-ins at the same restaurant where her mother had once worked in the kitchen. After her 1965 high-school graduation she went to Jackson, was arrested at a demonstration and held two weeks in the Jackson Fairgrounds compound (where Thelma Glover of Batesville famously dispersed a police standoff by walking into the line praying), then joined Curtis Hayes's Freedom Corps in the Delta paid by the National Council of Churches' Delta Ministry at $10 a week, working on the Mississippi Freedom Labor Union out of Rosedale alongside Owen Brooks, Isaac Foster, Homer Crawford, Joe Harrison, and Bill 'Billy' Kochiyama (whose Japanese American mother followed Malcolm X).

After two years (1966-68) at Bishop College in Texas, where her natural Afro was a small but conscious cultural statement, Henderson was brought back into movement work by Dr. King's spring 1968 visit to Batesville (organized through her uncle), then traveled with Leon Hall and SCLC's mule train from Selma through Montgomery, Birmingham, and on to Washington's Resurrection City. Disillusioned by the gap between SCLC's preachers staying at the Pitts Motor Inn while the poor lived in the mud of the National Mall, she left the Poor People's Campaign at Frank Smith's urging, became a student at the Institute for Policy Studies under Smith, and joined the staff of the Drum and Spear Bookstore, which she describes as the largest Black bookstore in the country, with Ralph Featherstone as a later manager. The store was teargassed during a Lerone Bennett Jr. book signing. Two days after the store moved to a larger location, Featherstone and 'Che' Payne were killed in a car bombing on March 10 [March 9], 1970, en route to H. Rap Brown's trial. Henderson then worked at the Black Child Development Institute under Florence Tate, traveled with Judy Richardson and Mimi (Curtis Hayes's then-wife) to Puerto Rico, Haiti, Trinidad, and Barbados as informal mental-health recovery, and in 1974 entered the Michele Clark Fellowship Program at Columbia University, beginning a 28-year newspaper career that started at the Daily Argus in Mount Vernon, New York.

Henderson also testifies to a parallel current of African cultural retention in northern Mississippi: every July 3rd-4th in her childhood, older men (Sid Hemphill's and later Othar Turner's tradition) played fife-and-drum music whose rhythms ethnomusicologist Alan Lomax and Smithsonian field recorders later traced back to West Africa (Mali). She also discloses family history she only pieced together decades later: her great-great-grandfather Duncan C. Henderson was a white slaveholder who owned 13 people including her great-great-grandmother (later identified as Classy Idae Lena); a 1952 family crisis in which her father, an uncle who had served in World War II, and a younger uncle confronted a group of white men in Pope, Mississippi resulted in one uncle being electrocuted by the state for a killing that her surviving California uncle eventually confessed (through his widow) he himself had committed; Henderson believes her father's death eleven months later was a poisoning. She closes with reflection on the present political moment, observing that the close-knit community that sustained Movement work no longer exists in the same form and that she finds the news 'too painful' to follow closely.
key_themes: Black landownership and Mississippi farm life
SNCC voter registration and the Mississippi Delta movement
Armed self-defense alongside nonviolent direct action
Generational and gender dynamics of the Mississippi movement
African cultural retention and the Mississippi fife-and-drum tradition
From organizing to journalism as a vocation of accountability
Family memory, white ancestry, and reconstructing identity
main_summary.historical_significance: Henderson's testimony is a rare first-person account of the youth-organizing layer of the Panola County Mississippi movement, which sat between the better-documented Greenwood and McComb projects, and of the post-1965 Delta Ministry Freedom Corps and Mississippi Freedom Labor Union — small organizations rarely covered in the dominant Movement historiography. Her account documents (a) the routine arming of older Black women in churches well before the public emergence of the Deacons for Defense; (b) the daily life of the 1968 Poor People's Campaign from the perspective of staff who saw the inequity between SCLC leadership and Resurrection City residents; (c) the operating culture of the Drum and Spear Bookstore in Washington as both commercial enterprise and movement institution; and (d) the survival of West African fife-and-drum music in northern Mississippi as recorded by Alan Lomax and the Smithsonian. As an oral history it also models how movement participants accumulated insight about their own family histories of resistance and white ancestry decades after the fact.

errors: The summary states Featherstone was killed on 'March 10' which is how Henderson dates it in the transcript; the corpus and historical record establish March 9, 1970. The summary preserves Henderson's recollection but bracket-clarifies the canonical date.

The summary attributes the Concord Missionary Baptist Church organ substitution ('Oh Freedom' for 'Amazing Grace') to Concord; the transcript names Concord as Henderson's family church after her mother's remarriage but the specific service where the substitution occurred is not explicitly named — minor framing imprecision.

The summary frames Henderson's two-week Jackson Fairgrounds detention as occurring in 1965 (the year Henderson gives elsewhere when discussing carrying a pistol); the transcript places it shortly after she turned 18 and graduated high school, with later cross-reference to '65' — the year alignment is consistent with the transcript but is a synthesis.
improvements: Add a citation-grounded sentence on the Lerone Bennett Jr. tear-gas incident with closer transcript-anchored framing rather than the generalized 'teargassed during a book signing.'
Differentiate Henderson's 1965 high school graduation date from her stated age 18 to remove the inference chain.
Move the speculative '[March 9]' bracket into a footnote-style aside rather than embedding inside the summary prose.

chapters.title: Childhood in Batesville and Brown's Cafe
chapters.summary: Henderson describes being born in Batesville, Mississippi (Panola County) to Lyora Morris and Hugo Henderson, both farmers on a small family farm that grew cotton, corn, okra, and soybeans. Her father died when she was about four or five 'under circumstances we're not sure of.' She grew up with her mother until age ten, when her mother remarried and the family moved to the farm. The land was on what the community called 'Section 7' — 80 acres owned by two groups of brothers across the road from each other. Before moving to the farm, Henderson lived in town and accompanied her mother to work at Brown's Cafe, where as a child she was given an apron 'all the way to the floor' and assigned to wash dishes in the back kitchen. At about age eight she carried a tray of glasses out front under her mother's instruction and saw white patrons eating in the dining room while Black patrons ate in the kitchen with her mother — her first awareness that segregation was structurally enforced.

improvements: Could explicitly name the surrounding North vs South Panola County distinction Henderson develops later.

chapters.title: Emmett Till, the NAACP Card, and the Kirkendall Incident
chapters.summary: At about age eight, Henderson encountered the Emmett Till murder through Jet and Look (or Life) magazines her mother bought her, crying more for Till's parents than for him. She later found her mother's NAACP card and was told 'you must never, ever say that again.' Her family church, West Camp Missionary Baptist, hosted Reverend Middleton Godfrey, who traveled in a 'little beat up Jeep' and delivered Black history lectures after services every other Sunday — Henderson notes she came to recite his Crispus Attucks references along with him. The chapter culminates in the Kirkendall incident, in which a local Sunday school superintendent's young daughter was accused by the sheriff of stealing and brought home for her father to beat her; he refused. The neighborhood's energy 'changed' — most of the Black men in the area walked toward the Kirkendall house with guns on their shoulders, sent the women and children away, and climbed into the trees to protect the family. The Kirkendalls moved to Indiana shortly after and Henderson never saw them again.
related_events: Armed Black self-defense in Panola County

improvements: Reverend Middleton Godfrey's first name is given as 'Reverend Middleton, God-free' in the transcript ASR; treating as 'Middleton Godfrey' is an interpretive choice that could be flagged.

chapters.title: Meeting Frank Smith and the Greenwood Voter Registration Workshop (June 1963)
chapters.summary: At Thanksgiving 1962, Henderson — then about 13 — met SNCC field secretary Frank Smith at her cousin's house across the road from her own. Smith had not come for movement work; he had met Henderson's cousin at Rust College in Holly Springs. Henderson, intrigued, climbed into his car and showed him the house where the uncle of one of Emmett Till's killers lived. She told her mother she had ridden with 'a stranger' — 'he's not a stranger, he's his friend' — and conveyed Smith's lesson about Black voting registration: at the time only about two Black people in Panola County were registered. By June 1963, with her uncle's go-ahead ('Jimmy's going'), Henderson, her cousin Jimmy, her friend Faye, and John Hardy traveled to Greenwood for a SNCC voter-registration workshop. She stayed at Mama Hackman's house, attended her first mass meeting (she at first confused 'mass M-A-S-S' with 'mask M-A-S-K'), and the next morning learned from a young man known as 'Freedom' that Medgar Evers had been killed the night before in Jackson. She canvassed for the first time, met Willie Peacock and James Peacock (his brother), John O'Neal (later of the Free Southern Theater), and learned to fill out voter-registration forms including Section 30 of the Mississippi Constitution interpretation.

improvements: Frank Smith is not in the ground-truth corpus; flag for potential corpus addition.

chapters.title: Returning to Batesville and the 1964 Summer Project
chapters.summary: Henderson returned from the Greenwood workshop and began trying to organize her family — 'I harassed my mother and my stepfather about registering to vote.' Her mother said 'I think those people hypnotized you. You went to Greenwood, you didn't come back the same person.' Her stepfather memorized Mississippi Constitution Section 30 ('he talked it all night long') and both her mother and stepfather registered to vote in 1964. Henderson describes a flash of resentment that her local Batesville neighborhood, which she and her friends had been working on for a year, suddenly came alive when the white summer-project volunteers arrived in 1964 — 'we've been begging you to do this for a year.' Her mother instructed her not to hang out with 'these white children' because 'they are going to leave and you're going to be here'; Henderson got in trouble for accepting a ride home from a Harvard volunteer named Jeff Cowan after a late COFO ('co-full' in the transcript ASR) office meeting. She also recounts another voter-registration meeting at West Camp where two suspicious white men inquired about what was happening; almost every woman in the church 'petted her purse' to indicate her concealed pistol. 'The women walked with pistols in their purses, even to church.'

improvements: The Harvard volunteer's name in the ASR is rendered 'Jeffrey "Jeff" Cowan' but is not independently verified against external records.

chapters.title: Lunch Counter Sit-ins, Jail, and Robert Miles
chapters.summary: Henderson describes a 1964 sit-in at the Batesville restaurant where her mother had worked. Her mother had already quit, saying 'if anybody puts their hands on you, I'm coming out of the kitchen with a knife.' About 14 young Black people, mostly girls, entered after nonviolent training. White farmers came in wearing brown cotton gloves, dragged them out by their seats, banged a couple of their heads together, and threw them into the street. The group tried a second restaurant — owners locked the door and pool-hall regulars came out with cues. Walking home, the group was arrested by a sheriff's deputy for 'breaking a city ordinance,' marched to jail singing freedom songs, but those under 18 (including Henderson) were released at 5 p.m. When older participants (including Chris Williams) went to trial, the community turned out in such numbers that a local man tried to assault one of the white volunteers in the crowd; Robert Miles, the West Camp landowner and local NAACP leader, 'decked him.' Miles was arrested; supporters had to put up their land to bail him out. Henderson reflects that her high-school principal told her 'if we can do anything to help, let you know,' indicating broader community support, and a deputy sheriff's odd visit to her house ('oh, wrong house') prompted her stepfather to load a shotgun in the corner.

improvements: Robert Miles is referenced but not in the ground-truth corpus.

chapters.title: Leaving for Mississippi Freedom Labor Union: Jackson Arrest and Fairgrounds Detention (1965)
chapters.summary: Henderson, having just turned 18 and graduated high school, declined her Mississippi Industrial College scholarship and went to a Mary Holmes College meeting where she reconnected with the 1963 SNCC people. Her older sister gave her $10. She caught a ride to Jackson with Lou Grant (then head of the local project) to Mount Beulah conference center, then to the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party office in Jackson. She passed out leaflets, was offered a place to stay by the last person leaving the office, and the next day was arrested within an hour of demonstrating. She was held two weeks in the Jackson Fairgrounds — 'about a thousand women' on concrete floors with thin mattresses, washing clothes by passing a single trench coat around. Most of the older Batesville women she had seen at Mount Beulah were also there. White women detainees were transferred to the city jail. One morning, Thelma Glover of Batesville walked into a standoff between cops with billy clubs and detainees and 'started praying and swinging her arms,' and by the time she finished, the officers had backed out. After bail (helped by a woman whose sister was a Black female police officer), Henderson connected with Hollis Watkins, traveled to McComb, met Curtis Hayes (later Curtis Muhammad), and went on to Cleveland, Mississippi to work in the Delta on the Mississippi Freedom Labor Union.

errors: The summary uses '1965' for the Jackson Fairgrounds detention based on Henderson's later reference to that year; the transcript places it shortly after her June 1965 high-school graduation, which is consistent but is an inference.
improvements: Cite the Hollis Watkins ground-truth corpus entry to confirm his SNCC role and McComb base.

chapters.title: Freedom Corps in the Delta: Rosedale and the Mississippi Freedom Labor Union
chapters.summary: Henderson joined Curtis Hayes's Freedom Corps, paid $10 a week by the National Council of Churches' Delta Ministry. Initially in Cleveland with Owen Brooks (whom she thought was 'old' at about 30), she was placed with the Frank Davis family in Rosedale — across the street from the courthouse and jail — where she shared a room with their 14-year-old daughter and also boarded with Morton Thomas from Boston (a Harvard student) and Bill 'Billy' Kochiyama, the Japanese-American son of Yuri Kochiyama (a Malcolm X follower) whose family had been interned during World War II. Billy was the first person to tell Henderson she was 'Afro-American'; he was also not a strict adherent of nonviolence and was arrested for kicking a Klansman who had called him 'Tojo' during a march. Henderson used her $10 fine money to keep him out of jail. The Freedom Corps members visited plantations to talk to farm workers about the Mississippi Freedom Labor Union, leaving a lookout in their van; when dust signaled the plantation owner approaching, they jumped into the van and fled. Workers who walked off plantations often had nothing — debts at the company store and nowhere to go. She also describes a Delta Pines plantation with seven churches. She tried to organize a small freedom school to teach adults to write their names. She was later sent by Delta Ministry to Minnesota, Pennsylvania (Pittsburgh area, where she discovered the realities of Northern segregation), and Illinois (Chicago) to fundraise — her first plane rides. Later in the chapter, she was arrested in Rosedale at a church-steps rally and taken to Parchman prison for an overnight; the staff forced detainees to drink an unknown liquid and gave them only their tops back after a strip search. Martin Thomas was beaten by other prisoners.
related_events: Delta Ministry of the National Council of Churches
Yuri Kochiyama and the post-internment Black-Asian alliance

errors: The summary identifies Yuri Kochiyama by her widely-known name; the transcript only refers to 'Billy's mom' as 'a big follower of Malcolm X.' This is an interpretive identification based on the documented Yuri Kochiyama / Bill Kochiyama family relationship and is grounded but represents a synthesis beyond the transcript itself.
improvements: Either cite the Yuri Kochiyama identification more explicitly as researcher inference or remove it; given the strong external evidence (Yuri + Bill Kochiyama are well-documented mother-son), partial support is the conservative call. Owen Brooks and Frank Davis are not in the corpus.

chapters.title: Bishop College and Natural Hair
chapters.summary: Henderson enrolled at Bishop College, a Baptist school in Texas, in 1966 with Marjorie Hyatt of Hattiesburg and Linda Wilson; Tom Brown, a former SNCC field secretary working in the admissions office, recruited them. The driver who picked them up from Dallas had 'Repent and be saved' on the side of his car. Henderson arrived with a freshly-cut Afro and was stared at by the other women students. She was fired from her first work-study job in the laundry for failing to use the rolling-drier correctly. A librarian told her she 'looked as bad as that African woman'; her friend Jamella Stokes retorted that she had never seen anyone born with blue hair before. Within two days other women on the dorm were asking her to cut their hair. The Ebony cover story 'All Natural' in 1966 made the style safer for her mother to accept ('see, Juadine's not the only one'). Henderson skipped chapel by sending her punch card by another student and was caught when the dean of women came to her room to find her 'playing the ukulele' (actually her guitar). On Sundays, instead of going to church, she visited Lafayette Locke at the Seagoville federal prison nearby, an aspiring writer her English professor had connected her with, signing out as if attending a Quaker meeting.

improvements: Bishop College itself is not in the ground-truth corpus; Tom Brown not verified.

chapters.title: Dr. King's Visit, the Mule Train, and Resurrection City
chapters.summary: After leaving Bishop in 1968, Henderson was at home in Batesville when her mother insisted she go see Dr. King speak there before his death; Henderson refused until her uncle came to get her. King had also spoken at the rally in Batesville at the end of the 1966 Meredith March. Shortly after, her friend Tate (whom she had known from Freedom Corps) arrived to organize for the Poor People's Campaign. Henderson went with Tate to Memphis, stayed at the Lorraine Motel where King had been assassinated, and agreed to help organize buses from Grenada with Leon Hall (an SCLC organizer). She did not intend to go to Washington. Leon Hall persuaded her to come to Selma, then Montgomery (staying with Hall's family), then Birmingham, then on to Washington with the mule train. In Washington, the staff stayed at the Pitts Motor Inn while the poor lived in tents on the National Mall — 'something unfair about this situation.' Driving down 14th Street one day she saw Charlie Cobb (Charles E. Cobb Jr.) and learned the old SNCC network was in Washington. Curtis Hayes and his wife Mimi came; the next day Frank Smith arrived and told Henderson 'get your stuff, let's go' — and she left Resurrection City and the Poor People's Campaign behind. In Birmingham she had befriended journalists Earl Caldwell (New York Times), Thomas A. Johnson (New York Times), and Robert C. 'Bob' Maynard (Washington Post), and decided journalism was interesting work.
related_events: Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. assassination (April 4, 1968)

improvements: Verify Leon Hall's SCLC role against external records.

chapters.title: Drum and Spear Bookstore, Featherstone Killing, and Sauti ya Watoto
chapters.summary: After leaving Resurrection City, Henderson got an apartment, became a student at the Institute for Policy Studies under Frank Smith (joined by Marvin Holloway and Ivanhoe Donaldson as fellows), and got a job at the Drum and Spear Bookstore — which she describes as one of the biggest Black bookstores in the country. Started by Charlie Cobb and a group called Afro-American Resources, the store stocked 'every book in the world you could find about black people.' Tony Gittens managed it first, then Ralph Featherstone — 'an organizer in Mississippi' — took over. The store was tear-gassed during a Lerone Bennett Jr. book signing. The store moved to a larger space, and two days later, on March 10, 1970 (the historical record places this on March 9), Featherstone and 'Che' Payne were 'blown up in a car.' Henderson notes the staff received no psychological counseling for this loss. The store was connected to the Center for Black Education (an offshoot of Federal City College) and to the Sunday-night radio program Sauti ya Watoto, in which Henderson and Mimi (Curtis Hayes's then-wife) read regular stories on the air, taking on character voices.
related_events: Ralph Featherstone and 'Che' Payne car bombing (March 9, 1970)

errors: The transcript reports Featherstone was killed on 'March 10th, 1970' but the canonical date in the ground-truth corpus is March 9, 1970 — the summary preserves Henderson's framing while bracketing the canonical date.
improvements: The 'Afro-American Resources' framing is the transcript's, but the corpus Drum and Spear entry credits 'Charlie Cobb, Courtland Cox, Ralph Featherstone, Curtis Hayes, Marvin Holloway, and Jennifer Lawson' as co-founders — could integrate this for richer attribution.

chapters.title: African Cultural Retention: The Mississippi Fife-and-Drum Tradition
chapters.summary: Henderson recounts that every July 3rd and 4th of her childhood, older Black men in Panola County played fife-and-drum music — a tradition she did not at the time know was African. The first sound was a 'big, big boom' on the third; everyone got new clothes; a baseball game between the visiting team and neighborhood men preceded the music; women cooked; only men danced to the drums, while the kids danced to the jukebox during breaks. Henderson was embarrassed by her stepfather and his brother dancing. She later learned the Smithsonian recorded these musicians in the 1940s and traced the rhythms to West Africa, specifically Mali. Othar Turner followed Sid Hemphill in this tradition; a plaque commemorates the lineage in northern Panola County. Alan Lomax wrote that women in other parts of the state saluted the drums and that the celebrations sometimes included slaughtered goats and goat sandwiches — not part of Henderson's local tradition, which featured normal picnic food and fish sandwiches.
related_events: Othar Turner and Sid Hemphill drum lineage

improvements: Othar Turner and Sid Hemphill are not in the ground-truth corpus; the Mali rhythm tracing is the transcript's own claim from online research.

chapters.title: Family History, Journalism, and Reflection
chapters.summary: Henderson discloses family history she only pieced together as an adult: her great-great-grandfather Duncan C. Henderson was a white slaveholder who owned 13 people including her great-great-grandmother (later identified as Classy Idae Lena via a cousin doing genealogy). Henderson recounts a 1952 family crisis in which an uncle returning from town in Pope, Mississippi was jumped by white men over having dated white women; her father and another uncle (a World War II veteran) came to his aid. Someone in the family was killed, another paralyzed; one uncle took responsibility — leaving his car in town as a decoy and turning himself in after the family was threatened. He was electrocuted by the state of Mississippi around 1952. Eleven months later her father died mysteriously, with the family privately believing he was poisoned, possibly via blood-pressure medicine from Dr. Martin. The family sold the farm and moved to Kingsport, Tennessee. Decades later, the surviving California uncle confessed (through his widow, after his death) that he was actually the one who had done the killing his brother was electrocuted for. Henderson reflects on her career: short stints at the Children's Defense Fund and IFCO (Interreligious Foundation for Community Organization), then at the National Urban Coalition (recruited again by Florence Tate), then in 1974 entering the Michele Clark Fellowship Program at Columbia University. Earl Caldwell pushed her to participate. Her first newspaper job was at the Daily Argus in Mount Vernon, New York; her first story was the front-page account of a child decapitated by a broken elevator. She worked as a newspaper journalist for 28 years. She closes that the present political moment is 'scary,' that 'at least then we had a close-knit community' that has since scattered, and that she finds the news 'too painful' to follow closely. She declines to offer advice to today's young people.
related_events: Michele Clark Fellowship Program at Columbia University
Mississippi family resistance and white ancestry

improvements: The 1952 family-crisis details (death, paralysis, electrocution date) are second-hand family history not externally verifiable; framing them as Henderson's reported family memory rather than as established fact is the right move.

interview_metadata.interviewee_title: Civil Rights Activist and Journalist; SNCC and Delta Ministry Freedom Corps Veteran
interview_metadata.interviewer_affiliation: Library of Congress / Civil Rights History Project

evidence: Opening establishes context immediately: 'I was born in Batesville, Mississippi, Panola County. My parents were Lyora Morris and Hugo Henderson. We were farmers.' (Segment 8, 00:00:46,000)

Chronologically organized from childhood through 1962 SNCC contact, 1963 Greenwood, 1964 sit-ins, 1965 Jackson, 1965-66 Freedom Corps, 1966-68 Bishop, 1968 Poor People's Campaign, 1968-1974 Drum and Spear, and on into 28-year journalism career (across multiple segments)
Reflection-ending arc: closes with refusal to give advice to today's youth ('I wish I did. I do not. Because I don't know') and reflects on community scattering. (Segments 2700-2718, 02:28:30,000)
story_structure_coherence.justification: Henderson moves through her life in a strong chronological arc that the interviewer reinforces with clarifying questions. Some sections backtrack — the church organ-substitution story is revisited mid-interview, and family-history disclosures come near the end rather than the beginning. The interview maintains overall coherence with a clear beginning (rural Mississippi childhood), middle (Movement work), and end (journalism career + present-day reflection).

most_vivid_moment.quote: And he came in and they started grabbing the seats we were on and slinging them across the floor and we were holding on. Like we're not falling yet. And then they started picking us up bodily and throwing us out into the street in front of cars or whatever. And they took a couple of people and banged their heads together.

evidence: Detailed scene at Brown's Cafe age eight: 'the next tray of glasses that you wash do not stop walking until you get out front with them.' (Segment 39, 00:03:08,000)

The Jackson Fairgrounds detail: 'somebody had a trench coat and we passed a trench coat around. You can go take a shower. You can wear a trench coat while you're taking your shower and washing your clothes... with ivory soap. For years I couldn't stand the smell of ivory soap because of that.' (Segments 960-964, 00:53:53,000)

Thelma Glover dispersing the standoff: 'walked in the middle of the little line and started praying and swinging her arms. And by the time she finished praying, they had all backed out.' (Segments 1019-1021, 00:57:18,000)

The plantation chase scene: 'when you saw the dust coming, you knew the owner was coming. So you had to beep the horn. And everybody ran out from wherever they were and jumped in the car and we took off.' (Segments 1181-1183, 01:06:24,000)
vivid_details_examples.justification: Henderson's recall is exceptionally specific — full names of dozens of people, exact ages (she repeatedly says 'I was eight'), concrete sensory anchors (the apron 'all the way to the floor,' the ivory soap, the dust kicked up by approaching plantation trucks). The reconstructed dialogue is plentiful and feels true to the speaker rather than embellished.

most_authentic_moment.quote: I cried for his parents more than him. Thinking what would mother do if somebody did that to me.

emotional_range: pride (her natural hair, Freedom Corps work)
vulnerability_elements: Admits being 'a little resentful' that her town came alive when white volunteers arrived
Confesses she was 'too young and too silly to worry' about the deputy sheriff's visit
Acknowledges deep difficulty with Ben Brown's killing and not handling it well
Discloses very recent family-history revelations including her uncle's deathbed-area confession through his widow
Closes by saying she 'tries not to pay attention' to today's politics because 'it's too painful'
emotional_authenticity.justification: Henderson is emotionally honest throughout — she names her own youthful naïveté, her own resentment toward latecomers to organizing, the unfinished grief for friends killed, and her current avoidance of news coverage. The range is wide but the delivery is mostly understated, more wry-funny than weeping. The interview hits genuine vulnerability at the Featherstone bombing memory ('we probably should have had some kind of psychological counseling, but we didn't') and at the close.

narrative_quality.overall_assessment: Henderson is a gifted storyteller whose chronological arc, vivid scenes, and emotional candor combine to create a high-engagement narrative. Some sections backtrack and the most painful disclosures (the 1952 family crisis, the Featherstone killing) arrive in the middle to late portions, which slightly weakens the structural arc. Her willingness to name her own ambivalence and limits — rather than offering tidy resolution — is one of the interview's most authentic features.

major_events: Greenwood SNCC voter-registration workshop, June 1963, with Medgar Evers assassination breaking during the workshop (Segment 371, 01:21:13,000 [original 00:21:13,000])
Direct organizing of Batesville/Panola County youth voter-registration before the 1964 Summer Project (Segments 466-480, 00:28:30,000)
Mississippi Freedom Labor Union organizing in Rosedale and the Bolivar/Sunflower/Washington County tri-county region (Segments 1086-1252, 01:00:00,000)
Poor People's Campaign and Resurrection City staff perspective, 1968 (Segments 1620-1708, 01:30:00,000)
Drum and Spear Bookstore staff during Featherstone bombing, March 1970 (Segments 1782-1789, 01:40:38,000)

key_figures.name: Hollis Watkins
key_figures.relationship: SNCC McComb contact who connected her to Curtis Hayes

key_figures.name: Robert Miles
key_figures.relationship: Local NAACP / movement leader, neighborhood elder before her family knew his movement work

key_figures.name: Charlie Cobb (Charles E. Cobb Jr.)
key_figures.relationship: SNCC contemporary, Drum and Spear co-founder

key_figures.name: Florence Tate
key_figures.relationship: Black Child Development Institute / National Urban Coalition mentor

key_figures.name: Earl Caldwell, Thomas A. Johnson, Bob Maynard
key_figures.relationship: Journalist mentors during 1968 Poor People's Campaign

key_figures.name: Dr. Martin Luther King Jr.
key_figures.relationship: Heard him speak Batesville 1968 and at end of 1966 Meredith March

organizational_involvement.organization: Delta Ministry / National Council of Churches Freedom Corps

organizational_involvement.role: Bus organizer (Grenada), Mule Train, Resurrection City staff

organizational_involvement.organization: Drum and Spear Bookstore / Center for Black Education
organizational_involvement.role: Bookstore staff, Sauti ya Watoto radio host

organizational_involvement.organization: Black Child Development Institute, IFCO, National Urban Coalition
Michele Clark Fellowship Program, Columbia University

unique_firsthand_perspective.unique_access: Henderson is one of relatively few testifiers who can speak first-person to the rarely-documented Mississippi Freedom Labor Union (Rosedale base, post-1965), the Delta Ministry Freedom Corps cohort, the Drum and Spear Bookstore staff during the Featherstone bombing, and the Mississippi fife-and-drum tradition that the Smithsonian recorded. She is also a witness to the gendered tension between SNCC's all-male Freedom Corps cohort and the women they were organizing alongside.
unique_firsthand_perspective.justification: Henderson sits across many institutional histories that are individually well-documented but rarely come together in a single life: the Greenwood SNCC project, the McComb network, the Mississippi Freedom Labor Union, the Poor People's Campaign, and the Drum and Spear Bookstore. Her perspective on each is insider-mid-rank, not leadership — which produces unusually rich operational detail about how the movement actually worked day-to-day.

precision_examples: Thanksgiving 1962 first contact with Frank Smith (Segment 215, 00:13:35,000)
June 1963 trip to Greenwood with Jimmy, Faye, John Hardy (Segment 327, 00:19:02,000)
Medgar Evers killed during the workshop, learned from 'a kid who called himself freedom' (Segment 371, 00:21:13,000)
$10 a week paid by Delta Ministry; Mississippi Industrial College scholarship declined (Segments 861, 1099)
Drum and Spear store address moved two days before Featherstone car bombing March 10 [March 9], 1970 (Segments 1782-1784, 01:40:38,000)
Smithsonian 1940s recording of Mississippi fife-and-drum traced to Mali (Segments 1887-1888, 01:47:08,000)
Michele Clark Fellowship Program at Columbia University, 1974 (Segments 2355-2358, 02:11:43,000)
verifiable_claims: Frank Smith met Juadine Henderson at her cousin's house in Batesville at Thanksgiving 1962
Henderson was at the Greenwood SNCC office the morning Medgar Evers was assassinated (June 12, 1963)
Ralph Featherstone was killed in a car bombing March 10 [March 9], 1970 — date in Drum and Spear context matches historical record within one day
Drum and Spear Bookstore was tear-gassed during a Lerone Bennett Jr. book signing
Sauti ya Watoto was a Sunday-night Washington-area radio program with movement staff
Michele Clark Fellowship Program at Columbia University in 1974 was a real program for minority journalists
specificity_of_details.justification: Henderson is precise about years (1962, 1963, 1964, 1965, 1966, 1968, 1970, 1974), wages ($10/week), ages (8, 13, 14, 18), and the named participants in each scene. Some details require external verification — the Featherstone date is one day off from the historical record (March 10 vs March 9, 1970), and a few names (Frank Davis, Tom Brown of Bishop College admissions, Lou Grant of the 1965 Jackson project) are not in the standard ground-truth corpus.

new_information: Detailed operational account of the Mississippi Freedom Labor Union from Rosedale, including the company-store-debt mechanism that bound workers to plantations
The Jackson Fairgrounds 1965 detention conditions from a women-detainee perspective (mass concentration, single trench coat passed around, Thelma Glover's prayer dispersing a police line)
Direct testimony of the SCLC vs Resurrection-City-residents accommodation inequity in 1968
Drum and Spear Bookstore staff perspective on the immediate aftermath of the Featherstone car bombing — 'we probably should have had some kind of psychological counseling, but we didn't'
Black women's routine armed self-defense in church (the West Camp pistol-purse scene)
Pre-emergence-of-Deacons-for-Defense armed Black male community response to a sheriff's threat (the Kirkendall incident)
understudied_aspects: Mississippi Freedom Labor Union and the Delta Ministry's Freedom Corps as an institution
Northern Panola County / Batesville movement work as distinct from the Greenwood and McComb projects
African cultural retention in northern Mississippi via the Othar Turner/Sid Hemphill fife-and-drum tradition
The transition from Movement work to journalism as a continuing accountability vocation
Black-Asian alliance and the Kochiyama family in Mississippi movement work
fills_historical_gaps.narrative_complications: Henderson's account complicates the conventional 'nonviolent movement' narrative by documenting the deep, normalized armed self-defense of older Black women and men in Mississippi alongside the youth-led nonviolent direct action. She also complicates the SCLC Poor People's Campaign narrative by naming the staff-vs-residents accommodation inequity at the Pitts Motor Inn.
fills_historical_gaps.historiographical_value: Historians of the Mississippi Movement post-1965, of the Mississippi Freedom Labor Union, of Drum and Spear, and of Black-press history in the 1970s could draw directly from this testimony. The fife-and-drum / Smithsonian / Lomax / Mali trace is also a valuable cultural-history thread.
fills_historical_gaps.justification: Henderson's testimony makes a genuine contribution to multiple understudied sub-fields (MFLU, Delta Ministry Freedom Corps, Drum and Spear staff history, Mississippi fife-and-drum survival). Where her account overlaps with well-documented events (Medgar Evers killing, Poor People's Campaign), she adds operational texture rather than corrective new facts.

historical_value.overall_assessment: Henderson's interview has high historical value because of the breadth of institutions she passed through and her insider-mid-rank perspective on each. She rarely speaks as a leader but consistently speaks from inside the operational guts of organizations, which produces unusually useful institutional history. The fife-and-drum thread is a bonus.

transformation_growth.transformation_arc: Henderson transforms from a Batesville farm girl who at age eight knew something was 'seriously wrong' but had no framework, to a 13-year-old movement curious one ('I think those people hypnotized you'), to an 18-year-old Freedom Corps organizer who walked off her college scholarship to go to the Delta with $10 and 'no clue,' to a 28-year newspaper journalist who learned to ask the questions she had been too shy to ask in her first reporting assignment. Her arc is identity-deepening rather than dramatic conversion.
key_turning_points: Reading the Jet/Look story of Emmett Till at age eight (Segment 90, 00:07:01,000)
Meeting Frank Smith Thanksgiving 1962 ('that was really the beginning') (Segment 247, 00:15:02,000)
Greenwood week with the Medgar Evers killing happening during it (Segment 371, 00:21:13,000)
Decision at 18 to walk off the Mississippi Industrial College scholarship and go to the Delta with $10 (Segment 882, 00:51:33,000)
Featherstone bombing March 10 [March 9] 1970 ('we probably should have had some kind of psychological counseling') (Segment 1783, 01:40:50,000)
1974 entry into the Michele Clark Fellowship Program at Columbia — discovery of journalism as her real vocation (Segment 2357, 02:11:54,000)
transformation_growth.before_after_contrast: The young Henderson 'didn't think about worrying' and was 'too young and too silly to worry' through routine danger; the older Henderson explicitly says 'now it's scary' and 'I try not to pay attention. It's too painful.' The youthful fearlessness gives way to a sober adult assessment that the close-knit community sustaining that fearlessness no longer exists.
transformation_growth.inspirational_quality: Henderson's matter-of-fact courage — declining a scholarship, going to the Delta with $10, playing Oh Freedom instead of Amazing Grace at age 16 — is genuinely inspiring without ever being self-aggrandizing. Her willingness in 2015 to say 'I wish I had advice for today's young people. I do not' is itself a kind of integrity.
transformation_growth.justification: The transformation arc is real but understated. Henderson does not narrate herself as having undergone a dramatic conversion; she narrates a steady accumulation of moves, jobs, and discoveries. The emotional arc is more about the cost — the friends killed, the community scattered — than about triumphal personal growth.

peak_moments.description: White farmers throwing teenagers out of the Batesville lunch counter, then sheriff arresting them on the walk home and them singing freedom songs all the way to jail
peak_moments.stakes: Physical violence, criminal record, parental fear

peak_moments.description: Thelma Glover walking into the police-line standoff at Jackson Fairgrounds, praying and swinging her arms until the officers backed out of the room
peak_moments.stakes: Direct physical clash with armed officers

peak_moments.description: Plantation chase scenes during Mississippi Freedom Labor Union organizing — lookouts, horn-honking, fleeing van
peak_moments.stakes: Discovery by plantation owners, beating, sharecropper retaliation against speakers

peak_moments.description: Featherstone car bombing two days after Drum and Spear's move; Henderson does not 'do very well with it'
peak_moments.stakes: Death of close colleague, organizational trauma, no counseling

peak_moments.description: Stepfather loading the shotgun in the corner after the deputy 'wrong-housed' the family
peak_moments.stakes: Imminent threat of being shot by police

conflict_types: internal moral conflict (going to Greenwood without permission)
tension_conflict_triumph.emotional_range: From terror (deputy at the door, Parchman bus) to dark humor (lunch counter scene, the wiping of her smile in juvenile court) to grief (Featherstone, Ben Brown)
tension_conflict_triumph.justification: Multiple high-drama scenes are richly drawn and the stakes are made explicit. Henderson tends to flatten the danger in retrospective tone ('I was too young and silly to worry'), which paradoxically intensifies the dramatic content for the listener.

universal_themes: the cost of family secrets (the 1952 killing, her father's death)
the discovery that one is part of a larger movement
the gap between leadership and rank-and-file (Pitts Motor Inn vs Resurrection City)
the difficulty of finding a vocation
relatable_struggles: Working in an inhospitable institution (Bishop College, the National Urban Coalition typing pool)
Being underestimated and outpaced by peers
Losing close friends in your twenties without psychological support
Reconciling family white ancestry with movement work
Aging into a political moment one finds painful
human_moments: Wiping the smile off her face and throwing it on the floor in juvenile court (Segment 732, 00:43:20,000)
Asking her uncle for advice on whether to go to Greenwood (Segment 315, 00:18:21,000)
Pretending to type so others wouldn't know she couldn't, then catching up on her real work at night (Segment 1797, 01:42:00,000)
Being embarrassed by her stepfather dancing to the drums at the picnic (Segment 1874, 01:46:21,000)
relatable_experiences.connection_points: Henderson's specifics — the Mississippi farm, the segregated kitchen, the Movement organizing — are deeply context-specific, but her emotional registers (youthful naïveté, late-life weariness, embarrassment at parents, the discovery of work one loves) cross context. The closing 'I do not have advice' is a register most listeners can recognize.
relatable_experiences.justification: The interview is highly relatable on emotional registers despite its tight contextual specificity. Henderson's wit and her willingness to name her own complicated feelings (resentment, embarrassment, fear, weariness) keeps the interview from feeling like institutional testimony only.

emotional_resonance.overall_assessment: Henderson's interview achieves real emotional resonance through her specificity, dark humor, and willingness to name her own limits. The transformation arc is understated rather than triumphal, which is one of the interview's most authentic features but slightly limits its inspirational charge for a general audience.

barriers: Some acronyms (SNCC, SCLC, COFO, MFDP, NAACP, IFCO) are not always defined inline
Some movement-specific names (Frank Smith, Curtis Hayes, Robert Miles) require the listener to know they were movement figures
The transcription occasionally has Whisper artifacts (e.g., 'co-full' for COFO, 'in double-acp' for NAACP) that the listener would not hear in the original audio but that affect text readability
language_clarity.justification: Henderson is an articulate speaker with strong narrative skills. She uses movement-specific acronyms and people-names without always defining them, which is normal for an oral history interview given to other movement-knowledgeable scholars but reduces accessibility for general audiences. Her speech has a slight regional cadence but no significant dialect barrier.

high_energy_sections: 00:57:18,000 - 00:58:00,000 (Thelma Glover dispersing the Jackson Fairgrounds standoff)
slow_sections: 01:51:00,000 - 01:55:00,000 (post-revisit to church organ-substitution context, some repetition)
02:00:00,000 - 02:09:00,000 (family-history disclosures move slowly with many short interviewer follow-ups)
narrative_flow.attention_sustainability: The interview at ~2:29 is long for general-audience listening but contains enough high-energy episodes scattered throughout to sustain attention for an engaged listener. The middle slows briefly during interview re-direction; the late family-history section regains energy but in a quieter register.
narrative_flow.justification: The pacing is generally well-managed, with dramatic peaks distributed across the interview. Emilye Crosby is a skilled interviewer who reorients without interrupting. Some sections are heavily back-and-forth between short answers and short questions, which slows the rhythm.

themes_connected: Black landownership and political agency in Mississippi
Armed self-defense as a routine complement to nonviolent direct action
The post-1965 movement (often understudied compared to 1960-65)
Gender dynamics and women's leadership in the rural Mississippi movement
African cultural retention in the American South
Family history of slavery, white ancestry, and intergenerational resistance
broader_themes_connection.contemporary_relevance: Henderson's closing observations about the scattering of community and the present political moment ('it's scary') directly bridge to contemporary concerns about civic mobilization, voter suppression, and democratic backsliding. Her testimony to armed Black women in church speaks directly to ongoing debates about armed defense and movement strategy.
broader_themes_connection.meaning_making: Henderson explicitly connects her personal experience to broader movement themes ('young people stood up to power in this country, and like they are doing now pretty much. And that you can do it. And yes, you lose a few on the way. But you can't let it stop you'). She also draws her family history into the present by acknowledging her white great-great-grandfather's slaveholding lineage.
broader_themes_connection.justification: Henderson is unusually explicit about connecting her personal arc to larger movements (the youth-led-movement tradition, the cultural-retention thread, the journalism-as-accountability vocation), and her closing meditation directly bridges to the present moment.

accessibility.overall_assessment: Henderson is highly accessible for movement-knowledgeable listeners and adequately accessible for general audiences with some patience. The interview's length and acronym-density are mild barriers; the storytelling quality, dramatic specificity, and explicit thematic connections compensate well.

engagement_scores.overall_assessment: This is an excellent oral history interview that combines breadth of institutional participation, depth of operational detail, vivid storytelling, and emotional candor. Henderson's testimony fills documented gaps in the post-1965 Mississippi movement, the Mississippi Freedom Labor Union, the Drum and Spear Bookstore staff history, and the African cultural-retention tradition of northern Mississippi. The interview is not dramatically structured but is consistently engaging, and Henderson's willingness to name her own limits — including declining to offer prescriptive advice — gives the interview an unusual integrity. The interview's primary limitations are length, acronym density for general audiences, and some quiet sections in the late middle.
key_strengths: Insider-mid-rank perspective on multiple movement institutions (SNCC, MFLU, SCLC, Drum and Spear) rarely covered in a single life history
Exceptional concrete detail and reconstructed dialogue throughout, especially in dramatic episodes (lunch-counter sit-in, Jackson Fairgrounds, plantation chases, Featherstone bombing aftermath)
Emotional honesty about youthful naïveté, resentment, fear, and present-day weariness, without ever lapsing into self-aggrandizement

Documents historically understudied sub-fields: post-1965 Delta Ministry Freedom Corps, Mississippi Freedom Labor Union, Mississippi fife-and-drum African retention, Drum and Spear staff perspective on Featherstone bombing
Explicitly bridges personal experience to broader movement themes and contemporary relevance
key_limitations: Length of ~2:29 with some slow middle sections may limit general-audience attention sustainability
Frequent use of unexplained acronyms (SNCC, SCLC, COFO, MFDP, IFCO) and people-names assumes movement-history knowledge
Featherstone bombing date in transcript is given as March 10 (canonical date is March 9, 1970) — minor but a verification flag

claims.claim: Juadine Henderson was born in Batesville, Mississippi, Panola County, to Lyora Morris and Hugo Henderson, both farmers.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Yes, I was born in Batesville, Mississippi, Panola County. My parents were Lyora Morris and Hugo Henderson. We were farmers. (Segments 8-9, 00:00:46,000)
claims.rationale: Direct first-person testimony with exact names matching.

claims.claim: Her father died when she was about four or five.
claims.supporting_excerpt: My dad died when I was about four or five. We're not sure of all the circumstances. (Segment 11, 00:01:11,000)
claims.rationale: Direct first-person testimony.

claims.claim: She grew up on an 80-acre family farm her family called 'Section 7.'
claims.supporting_excerpt: It was 80 acres and one group of brothers owned one side of the road... And it was called Section 7. (Segments 24-26, 00:02:15,000)
claims.rationale: Direct testimony establishes both acreage and name.

claims.claim: As a child she worked at her mother's job at Brown's Cafe.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Before we moved to the farm my mom worked in a restaurant in town. It was called Brown's Cafe. And I would go in on Saturdays to be with her. (Segments 33-35, 00:02:43,000)
claims.rationale: Direct testimony with exact restaurant name.

claims.claim: At about age eight she learned about Emmett Till's murder through Jet and Look (or Life) magazines.
claims.supporting_excerpt: I think it was when the Emmett Till murder happened. And my mom bought me a copy. I think it was Look Our Life magazine. I'm not sure. And Jet Magazine. (Segments 90-94, 00:07:01,000)
claims.rationale: Direct testimony with the speaker's own uncertainty about Look vs Life preserved in the framing.

claims.claim: Her family attended West Camp Missionary Baptist Church, where Reverend Middleton Godfrey delivered Black history lectures after services.
claims.supporting_excerpt: West Camp Missionary Baptist Church... There was an older minister, Reverend Middleton, God-free, that's his name. And I hated to see him coming on Sundays because it meant we had to stay at church, another half an hour to listen to black history. He traveled around in a little bit up Jeep and he talked history. (Segments 157, 146-150)
claims.rationale: Church name directly named; minister name is ASR-rendered as 'Middleton, God-free' but the speaker confirms it as 'his name.'

claims.claim: Henderson met SNCC field secretary Frank Smith at her cousin's house at Thanksgiving 1962.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Frank Smith, the SNCC person, came to town in 1962. The Thanksgiving. He didn't come to do movement work. And my cousin, he met my cousin at Rust College, Holly Springs. (Segments 214-218, 00:13:35,000)
claims.rationale: Direct testimony with date and circumstance.

claims.claim: In June 1963 she went with her cousin Jimmy, friend Faye, and John Hardy to a SNCC voter-registration workshop in Greenwood, Mississippi.
claims.supporting_excerpt: So we headed to Greenwood, Mississippi. June, 1963. It was my cousin, Jimmy. My friend, Fay. John Hardy. (Segments 326-330, 00:18:59,000)
claims.rationale: Direct testimony with exact date and companions.

claims.claim: She learned of Medgar Evers's assassination the morning after arriving in Greenwood from a young SNCC worker known as 'Freedom.'
claims.supporting_excerpt: And then a kid who called himself freedom... Came in and we said, what's going on freedom? Why is everybody so sad? And he said, Medgar Evans was killed last night in Jackson. (Segments 367-371, 00:21:00,000)
claims.rationale: Direct testimony; Whisper renders 'Evers' as 'Evans' but the historical reference is clearly to Medgar Evers.

claims.claim: She heard that Fannie Lou Hamer, June Johnson, and Euvester Simpson were missing, and that Lawrence Guyot was sent to look for them.
claims.supporting_excerpt: June Johnson, who is from Greenwood, (Fannie Lou) Hamer who is from Ruleville, Euvester Simpson, who was from Itta Bena, I think, came up missing. And they sent Lawrence Guyot. off to look for them. (Segments 431-433, 00:26:32,000)
claims.rationale: Direct testimony naming all four figures and the sequence of events. The ground-truth corpus confirms all four are documented Winona-cohort figures.

claims.claim: Her stepfather memorized Section 30 of the Mississippi Constitution and both parents registered to vote in 1964.
claims.supporting_excerpt: My stepfather had to interpret Section 30 of the Mississippi Constitution. He memorized the thing... They were very proud of themselves. They both passed. (Segments 513-520, 00:32:30,000)
claims.rationale: Direct testimony with the constitutional-test section identified.

claims.claim: At a Batesville sit-in in 1964, white farmers wearing brown cotton gloves dragged Henderson and others out of the restaurant.
claims.supporting_excerpt: After the sheriff came in, some farmers came in putting on their brown cotton gloves... And they came in and they started grabbing the seats we were on and slinging them across the floor (Segments 614-621, 00:37:47,000)
claims.rationale: Direct testimony with sensory specificity (the gloves).

claims.claim: Robert Miles, a Panola County NAACP / movement leader, struck a man who tried to attack white volunteers during a trial.
claims.supporting_excerpt: And Robert Miles, our local hero, decked him. The guy who was going to go through the crowd to get Chris got hit by Robert Miles. (Segments 707-709, 00:42:05,000)
claims.rationale: Direct testimony with named participants.

claims.claim: At a West Camp Missionary Baptist voter-registration meeting, almost every woman 'petted her purse' to indicate her concealed pistol.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Almost every woman in the church petted her purse. Got mine right here. And the response was, carry on. (Segments 810-812, 00:46:18,000)
claims.rationale: Direct first-person observation.

claims.claim: Henderson was arrested in Jackson in 1965 and held two weeks in the Jackson Fairgrounds with about a thousand other women.
claims.supporting_excerpt: And taken to the Jackson Fairgrounds. And that's where all the old women I had seen from Batesville were. Everybody, there were about a thousand women in there. You know, I was in there for two weeks. (Segments 954-957, 00:53:33,000)
claims.rationale: Direct testimony confirms the location, duration, and approximate detainee count. The '1965' date is the speaker's later self-reference; the transcript places it shortly after she turned 18 and graduated high school, consistent with 1965 but inferentially derived.

claims.claim: Thelma Glover of Batesville dispersed a police-line standoff at the Jackson Fairgrounds by walking into the middle of the line, praying and swinging her arms.
claims.supporting_excerpt: And a lady from Batesville, Mrs. Thelma Glover, walked in the middle of the little line and started praying and swinging her arms. And by the time she finished praying, they had all backed out. (Segments 1018-1021, 00:57:15,000)
claims.rationale: Direct testimony with named participant and exact action.

claims.claim: Henderson joined Curtis Hayes's (Curtis Muhammad's) Freedom Corps at $10 a week, paid by the National Council of Churches' Delta Ministry, working in Cleveland and Rosedale, Mississippi.
claims.supporting_excerpt: And I stayed with Owen Brooks initially, because the National Council of Churches was paying the stipend for Freedom Corps... And we got $10 a week. (Segments 1086, 1099-1100, 01:00:57,000 - 01:01:33,000)
claims.rationale: Direct testimony with payment amount, organization, and location named.

claims.claim: She boarded in Rosedale with the Frank Davis family along with Morton Thomas from Boston (a Harvard student) and Bill 'Billy' Kochiyama.
claims.supporting_excerpt: I lived with a family there, Frank Davis, who was the local leader... Morton Thomas from Boston, I think, and a student at Harvard. Billy Kochiyama, who was Japanese grew up in Harlem. (Segments 1125-1131, 01:03:21,000)
claims.rationale: Direct testimony with named housemates.

claims.claim: Billy Kochiyama was the first person to tell Henderson she was 'Afro-American' and his mother was a follower of Malcolm X.
claims.supporting_excerpt: And his mom was a big follower of Malcolm X. And Billy was the first person who told me I was African-American. I think he called us Afro-American at the time. (Segments 1228-1230, 01:09:06,000)
claims.rationale: Direct testimony; the identification of Billy's mother as Yuri Kochiyama is external inference but well-documented in the broader historical record.

claims.claim: Henderson enrolled at Bishop College in Texas after Freedom Corps and arrived with a freshly-cut Afro.
claims.supporting_excerpt: I stayed at Bishop for two years... I had an afro body and had cut my hair. Low and all the little women girls were staring like, look, she washed her hair. (Segments 1511-1459, 01:25:01,000 - 01:21:58,000)
claims.rationale: Direct testimony with specific scene detail.

claims.claim: Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. spoke at Batesville in spring 1968 before his death, and at the end of the 1966 Meredith March.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Dr. King was coming to Batesville to speak before his death... In 1966, the Meredith March, he had been one of the people who came at the end. And he spoke at the rally. (Segments 1555, 1584-1585, 01:27:34,000 - 01:28:44,000)
claims.rationale: Direct testimony with two specific occasions named.

claims.claim: Henderson traveled with the SCLC mule train from Selma through Montgomery, Birmingham, and to Washington with the Poor People's Campaign in 1968.
claims.supporting_excerpt: And went to Selma... Come with us to Montgomery. And then you can go home. I stayed with Leon's family in Montgomery. Come with us to Birmingham. And then you can go home. And I ended up in Washington... With the Poor People's Campaign. (Segments 1618-1629, 01:30:00,000)
claims.rationale: Direct testimony with the sequence of cities named.

claims.claim: SCLC staff stayed at the Pitts Motor Inn while Poor People's Campaign residents lived in tents in the mud on the National Mall — an inequity Henderson explicitly noted.
claims.supporting_excerpt: SCLC staff, oh, this is terrible, I didn't had rooms at the Pitts Motor Inn... You have people living out here in mud because it was a muddy place. And we are all sitting up in the pit smoder in, eating, and sleeping, warm, warm showers. It's something unfair about this situation. (Segments 1664, 1671-1673, 01:34:18,000 - 01:34:54,000)
claims.rationale: Direct first-person observation including her explicit naming of the unfairness.

claims.claim: Henderson worked at the Drum and Spear Bookstore, started by Charlie Cobb and Afro-American Resources; Ralph Featherstone later managed it; the store was tear-gassed during a Lerone Bennett Jr. book signing.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Drum and Spear Bookstore was started, I guess, by Charlie. And a group called Afro-American Resources... Ralph Featherstone, who had been an organizer in Mississippi, came to be the manager of the store... Lerone Bennett Jr, who wrote before the Mayflower, was in the store signing books when the police tear gased the bookstore. (Segments 1727-1728, 1770, 1755-1757, 01:36:35,000 - 01:39:43,000)
claims.rationale: Direct testimony with named founders, manager, and event. Cobb and Featherstone are confirmed in the ground-truth corpus as Drum and Spear co-founders along with Curtis Hayes, Marvin Holloway, Jennifer Lawson, and Courtland Cox.

claims.claim: Ralph Featherstone and 'Che' Payne were killed in a car bombing on March 10, 1970, two days after the Drum and Spear move.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Then the day that two days after we moved to this bigger location, Featherstone was blown up in a car with [Che] Payne... That was 1970, March 10th. (Segments 1782-1784, 01:40:38,000 - 01:40:53,000)
claims.rationale: Direct testimony of the date and circumstance; the historical record (per the ground-truth corpus entry) places the bombing on March 9, 1970, one day before Henderson's stated date. The summary preserves Henderson's date with a bracketed clarification.

claims.claim: In her childhood, older men in Panola County played fife-and-drum music every July 3rd-4th, a tradition the Smithsonian recorded in the 1940s and that Alan Lomax traced to West Africa / Mali.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Two days, every July, third and fourth, there were these old men who played drums... The Smithsonian recorded them in the 1940s. Trace the rhythms that they were playing back to West Africa, Mali... Alan Lomax wrote that the women in other parts of the state saluted the drums. (Segments 1860, 1887-1888, 1901, 01:45:31,000 - 01:47:55,000)
claims.rationale: Direct testimony. The Mali tracing is the speaker's recollection of online research she did decades later; Othar Turner and Sid Hemphill are the named musicians she identifies.

claims.claim: Henderson entered the Michele Clark Fellowship Program at Columbia University in 1974 and began a 28-year newspaper career at the Daily Argus in Mount Vernon, New York.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Columbia University had a program for minority journalists. And I applied and got accepted. So I went off to New York in 1974 to the Michele Clark Fellowship Program at Columbia... my first newspaper job at the end of the summer in Mount Vernon, New York, working for the Daily Argus... And I did the newspaper stuff for 28 years. (Segments 2355-2358, 2398-2407, 02:11:43,000 - 02:14:48,000)
claims.rationale: Direct testimony of program name, year, first newspaper, and career duration.

claims.claim: Henderson's great-great-grandfather Duncan C. Henderson was a white slaveholder who owned 13 people, including her great-great-grandmother (later identified as Classy Idae Lena).
claims.supporting_excerpt: So I looked up the slave schedule and there he is, Duncan C. Henderson. And he owned 13 people... And she said, yes, I'm the great-great-granddaughter of Classy, Idae Lena. (Segments 2279-2280, 2291-2293, 02:08:17,000 - 02:08:57,000)
claims.rationale: Direct testimony with specific name and slaveholder-schedule lookup detail.

publication_decision.rationale: Main summary accuracy_score (86) is below the 90 threshold and citation_audit reports 2 partially_supported claims (the Jackson Fairgrounds 1965 date inferred from speaker self-reference, and the Featherstone bombing date which the speaker gives as March 10 while the canonical historical record is March 9, 1970). Quality_score (90) meets the threshold. The summary contains no unsupported (invented) claims and the two partial-support flags are both date-precision issues that a human reviewer can either confirm via cross-reference (Featherstone is well-documented as March 9, 1970 in the ground-truth corpus entry) or accept as the speaker's own framing with editorial bracketing. The fail-closed gate routes this to human review, which is the correct destination — a Smithsonian/LoC reviewer can quickly confirm the Featherstone date discrepancy is the speaker's own one-day-off recollection of a well-documented event, not a hallucination, and either publish with editorial annotation or have the speaker reconfirm. The chapter-level summaries are uniformly strong (each scores accuracy 86-95 and quality 86-90) and the citation_audit shows zero invented claims across 28 audited claims.
